UNIT 2 Project

Lylyan Yenson

February 2026

Lecture 06

# echo=TRUE shows code
# warning/message hide noisy output
# fig.width/height control plot size

Q1

Biscoe has the most penguins.

Adding theme_minimal() makes the graph simpler by getting rid of space below and above the penguin counts.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = island)) + geom_bar(fill = "steelblue") + labs(title = "Penguin Counts by Island", x = "Island", y = "Number of Penguins") + theme_minimal()

Q2

The general shape of the distrubution appears to be bimodal. Changing the bin number changes the size of the bars on the histogram with bin = 10 making the bars extremely thin and bin = 60 making the bars wider and better visible.

There are a larger number of Adelie penguins compared to Chinstrap and Gentoo. They have different approximate means.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 60, fill = "steelblue") + labs(x = "Body Mass (g)", y = "Number of Penguins" ) + facet_wrap(~ species) + theme_minimal()

Q3

Gentoo has the longest flippers.

It doesn’t necessarily change readability, but specifies on gender weight among species.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(x = "Species", y = "Flipper Length (mm)") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g, fill = species)) + geom_boxplot() + labs(x = "Gender", y = "Body Mass (g)") + theme_minimal()

Q4

Initially, the relationship looks unclear. After separating by species, there is a positive relationship between bill length and depth. The line does fit the data well.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) + geom_point() + labs(x = "Bill Length (mm)", y = "Bill Depth (mm)") + theme_minimal() + geom_smooth(method = "lm")
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Q5

All 3 species show a strong positive linear relationship between flipper length and body mass. Gentoo seems to be stronger than the others.

Males seem to have higher body mass and flipper length compared to females. There seems to be a consistent positive linear relationship between flipper length and body mass across all graphs.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(~species) + labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)", y = "Body Mass (g)") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) + geom_point() + facet_wrap(sex~species) + labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)", y = "Body Mass (g)") + theme_minimal()

Q6

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = island)) + geom_bar() + facet_grid(~ sex) + labs(x = "Island", y = "Penguins") + theme_minimal()

Q7

The most fun part of this exercise was playing around with the graphs. I enjoyed question 6 because it allowed me the most freedom to experiment with the different graphs and options. The amount of options was a bit difficult because there are a lot.

Lecture 07

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

Q1

The first line searches for the pattern “adelie” in the species_messy column of the penguins_messy data frame. The second line replaces all of the question marks from the sex_messy column of the penguins_messy data frame. The third line condenses the column to look neater and gets rid of unnecessary spaces in front of or after the label for the column island_messy from the penguins_messy data frame.

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie")
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?","")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [343] "M" "f"
str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)

Q2

316

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}")) 

sum(penguins_messy$valid_id)
## [1] 316

Q3

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  filter(valid_id == TRUE)

Q4

2024-01-14, 2024-01-17, 2024-01-08, 2024-01-26, 2024-01-06, 2024-01-08, 2024-01-08, 2024-01-19, 2024-01-14, 2024-01-24

PAL-535, PAL-442, PAL-668, PAL-920, PAL-223, PAL-899, PAL-268, PAL-139, PAL-847, PAL-238

penguins_messy %>% 
  mutate(
  file_date = str_extract(file_name, "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}"), 
  file_id = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}")
  )

Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(island = str_squish(island_messy))

unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>%
  mutate(sex = sex_messy %>%
           str_replace_all( "\\?","") %>%
           str_to_upper())

unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% 
  mutate(
    bill_mm = notes %>%
      str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm","") %>%
      na_if("") %>%
      as.numeric()
  )
## Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
## ℹ In argument: `bill_mm = `%>%`(...)`.
## Caused by warning in `notes %>% str_replace_all("(.*bill=)|mm", "") %>% na_if("") %>% as.numeric()`:
## ! NAs introduced by coercion
unique(penguins_messy$bill_mm)
##   [1] 39.1 39.5 40.3   NA 36.7 39.3 38.9 39.2 34.1 42.0 37.8 41.1 38.6 34.6 36.6
##  [16] 38.7 42.5 34.4 46.0 37.7 35.9 38.2 38.8 35.3 40.6 40.5 37.9 37.2 40.9 36.4
##  [31] 42.2 37.6 39.8 36.5 40.8 36.0 44.1 37.0 39.6 37.5 42.3 40.1 35.0 34.5 41.4
##  [46] 39.0 35.7 41.3 35.5 41.8 33.5 39.7 45.8 42.8 36.2 42.1 42.9 37.3 36.3 36.9
##  [61] 38.3 34.0 38.1 33.1 43.2 41.0 45.6 42.7 41.5 38.5 43.1 35.6 40.2 32.1 40.7
##  [76] 46.1 50.0 48.7 45.4 46.7 43.3 46.8 49.0 45.5 48.4 49.3 49.2 46.2 50.2 45.1
##  [91] 46.5 46.3 44.5 47.8 48.2 47.3 59.6 49.1 42.6 44.4 44.0 49.6 45.3 50.5 43.6
## [106] 44.9 45.2 46.6 48.5 50.1 45.0 43.8 50.4 45.7 54.3 49.8 49.5 43.5 50.7 47.7
## [121] 46.4 48.6 51.1 52.5 47.4 50.8 43.4 51.3 47.5 52.1 52.2 49.4 46.9 55.9 47.2
## [136] 41.7 53.4 48.1 51.5 55.1 48.8 49.9 52.7 51.7 47.0 52.0 45.9 50.3 58.0 42.4
## [151] 50.6 52.8 54.2 51.0 49.7 47.6 53.5 50.9 51.4 51.9 55.8

Q8

The most fun part of this exercise was when I got my codes to successfully run and give me the answers I wanted. The most confusing part was having to run many different lines which sometimes caused small errors like forgetting a parenthesis or a bracket. I have a general idea of how regular expressions are used.

Lecture 08

Q1

344 changed to 333

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)

nrow(penguins)
## [1] 333
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

Q2

363 changed to 333

Duplicates can mess up summary statistics because it can skew the data and give inaccurate results. It causes certain values to show repeatedly in the data.

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>%
  bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n = 30, replace = TRUE))

penguins_dup %>%
  distinct() %>% nrow()
## [1] 333
nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363

Q3

8

penguins_dataset <- penguins_clean %>%
  select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))

ncol(penguins_dataset)
## [1] 8

Q4

penguins_clean %>% 
  select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(ends_with("_mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>%
  select(matches("^(bill|flipper)"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

Q5

Across(where(is.numeric)) is better than typing out all numeric columns because it decreases the mistakes we can make while typing and simplifies our data. If we didn’t know their names, but we knew their type this function makes it much simpler.

penguins %>%
  drop_na %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarize(across(where(is.numeric), mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

Q6

penguins_clean <- penguins_clean %>%
  mutate(
    bill_length = case_when(
      bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short",
      bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium",
      bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long"
    )
  )

unique(penguins_clean$bill_length)
## [1] "short"  "medium" "long"

Q7

12 columns in penguins_instrumented and 9 columns in penguins_clean.

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)

penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>%
  drop_na(bill_length_mm) %>%
  mutate(instrument_id = case_when(
    year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A",
    year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B",
    year == 2009 ~ "caliper_c"
  )) %>%
  left_join(instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")

Q8

The most fun part of this exercise was reusing functions we already knew with new ones we just learned. The most difficult part was making sure everything was typed out correctly as we used a lot of functions within one run through. These general “survival tips” will definitely be useful in our data in the future as it helps us to simplify, better read, and add to our data.
